items.title: Linear regression as the foundation
items.description: Polynomial regression keeps the same coefficient-fitting machinery as linear regression; it only changes the feature matrix by adding powers of x.

items.title: Model complexity and overfitting
items.description: Each increase in polynomial degree adds parameters and variance; the bias-variance tradeoff explains why high-degree polynomials chase training noise.

items.title: Feature creation via powers
items.description: Raising inputs to integer powers (and optional interactions) is a deliberate feature-engineering step that makes curvature representable to a linear solver.

items.title: Scaling before expansion
items.description: Standardizing inputs before computing x^2, x^3, ... prevents large raw values from dominating the design matrix and stabilizes coefficient estimates.
